mnemonics.title: Memory Management Goals
mnemonics.content: MEMORIZE: Maximize processes, Enable sharing, Maintain protection, Organize efficiently, Reduce fragmentation, Increase utilization, Zero conflicts, Enable virtual memory

mnemonics.title: Address Binding Types
mnemonics.content: CLA: Compile-time (absolute), Load-time (relocatable), Execution-time (dynamic) - think CLAUDE as the binding assistant

mnemonics.title: Paging Components
mnemonics.content: PFT: Pages (logical), Frames (physical), TLB (translation lookaside buffer for fast translation)

acronyms.usage: Hardware that translates logical addresses to physical addresses and enforces memory protection
A cache of recent page table entries used to speed up address translation
Primary volatile memory that stores currently executing programs and data
Non-volatile memory containing firmware that boots the system
Allows devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the oldest page in memory
Page replacement algorithm that evicts the least recently accessed page

visualTips.visualization: Think of logical address as a room number in a hotel (building = page number, room = offset) and physical address as the actual GPS coordinates. The page table is like a hotel directory mapping room numbers to actual locations.
Imagine a puzzle with fixed-size pieces (pages) that can fit into any slot (frame) on a table (physical memory). The page table is your guide showing which piece is in which slot.
Think of a document with chapters (segments): each chapter has a name and specific length. You can place chapters anywhere but they're kept as distinct logical units.
Imagine a library's storage room (secondary storage) and desks (RAM). You only bring books (pages) to the desk when you need to read them, returning them to storage when done.

analogies.analogy: Memory management is like a hotel manager allocating rooms to guests. The manager must keep track of which rooms are occupied, allow guests to use only their assigned rooms, and efficiently use available space.
Swapping is like moving a student from one classroom to another - they leave their materials in their original desk and bring only what they need to the new location.
A page fault is like requesting a book from a library that isn't on your desk - you must go to the shelves (disk) to get it, which takes much longer than reading from your desk (RAM).
Thrashing is like a student spending all their time going back and forth to their locker (disk) to get materials instead of actually studying (computing).
